episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Working Lands & Industry
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Working Lands & Industry.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [CBC Arts] Some artists feeling 'blue dot fever,' fans say, as high ticket prices make concertgoers more selective and [Business Insider] New US Air Force investigation shows how an in-flight refueling of an F-22 Raptor went wrong to the tune of nearly $10 million, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Does cheap local AI hardware deployment change the competitive advantage for small-scale working-lands producers, or do governance and infrastructure barriers prevent them from capturing that benefit? Topics include: Local versus cloud AI deployment economics and cost curves, Data sovereignty and governance in producer co-operatives, Land tenure and investment horizon differences between Crown leases and title-holding Nations, Producer association governance and succession gaps between older and younger operators, Regional infrastructure pilots versus permanent structural funding for agricultural extension.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Food Policy Council

articles.title: [CBC Arts] Some artists feeling 'blue dot fever,' fans say, as high ticket prices make concertgoers more selective
articles.summary: The name "blue dot fever" comes from the blue dots on Ticketmaster's website, indicating unsold seats. Some fans say they're going to fewer shows because of the high price tag.

articles.title: [TechRadar] Why security leaders are cautious about agentic AI
articles.author: https://www.techradar.com/author/howie-koh
articles.summary: Agentic AI can improve security operations, but only with strong oversight and real context.

discussion.central_question: Does cheap local AI hardware deployment change the competitive advantage for small-scale working-lands producers, or do governance and infrastructure barriers prevent them from capturing that benefit?
discussion.riley_position: Local AI inference hardware is now affordable enough to be accessible to small producers, but the real barriers are governance, data infrastructure, and producer association priorities—which are solvable political economy problems rather than technical ones.
riley_key_evidence: Local AI inference setup costs $1,000-$3,000, less than a GPS spray controller
Quebec's agricultural extension model includes technology transfer functions that BC cut
Ontario commodity boards fund technology services through direct producer levies rather than grants
discussion.casey_position: Technology accessibility doesn't automatically translate to value capture for small producers; governance structures, land tenure insecurity, and support ecosystem gaps mean cheap tools risk further consolidating advantage to larger operations.
casey_key_evidence: Crown tenure leases create investment uncertainty that prevents capital reallocation similar to what outright-owning ranchers can do
Extension services in BC have been 'cut to the bone' leaving no support ecosystem for producers to assemble datasets from public sources
Technology navigator positions keep landing as grant-funded pilots lasting one cycle rather than permanent structural services
discussion.resolution: They agreed the technology window is open but the governance structures aren't ready, and the decision point matters now before large operators entrench data advantages—neither position fully conceded but they converged on the need for shared infrastructure and explicit data governance before consolidation occurs.
topics_covered: Local versus cloud AI deployment economics and cost curves
Data sovereignty and governance in producer co-operatives
Land tenure and investment horizon differences between Crown leases and title-holding Nations
Producer association governance and succession gaps between older and younger operators
Regional infrastructure pilots versus permanent structural funding for agricultural extension
